metadata.role: Canonical caution/look-alike mushroom records loaded with the normal mushroom chunks.
metadata.purpose: Canonical caution mushroom records that are not already present in the main mushroom chunks.

records.poisoning_effects: Contains amatoxin-type danger; poisoning can progress to severe liver injury and death. Treat any small brown wood-growing mushroom in this group as unsafe unless expert-confirmed.
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat. This is a deadly look-alike group, included here only as a caution record.
records.field_identification: Small brown gilled mushroom on wood or buried wood, often clustered; rusty brown spore print; brown to tawny cap that is moist when fresh and fades with age; thin ring or ring zone may be present but can disappear.
records.overview: Caution stub for the Galerina marginata group because it can be confused with edible wood-growing mushrooms such as Velvet Foot by casual foragers.
records.look_alike_notes: Separate from Velvet Foot and other edible wood mushrooms by spore print, ring/ring-zone clues, cap texture, season, and expert-level confirmation. Do not use this app as the deciding ID for small brown mushrooms.

ring: Thin ring or ring zone may be present
mushroom_profile.season_note: Often spring and fall in temperate regions, but possible across a broad season when conditions fit.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Galerina marginata
use_links.notes: Wood growth, rusty brown spore print, small brown cap, and ring/ring-zone clues.

records.mushroom_card_note: Deadly brown gilled wood mushroom; never confuse with velvet foot, honey mushrooms, or small edible brown caps.
records.edibility_detail: Deadly poisonous amatoxin-containing small brown mushroom. Do not eat under any circumstance. This record is included because Galerina marginata can grow on wood in clusters and can be confused with small edible wood-loving mushrooms such as velvet foot by careless foragers; cooking, drying, or freezing should not be treated as making it safe.
records.toxicity_notes: Potentially fatal amatoxin poisoning risk; treat every suspected Galerina marginata as deadly. Key practical separation from edible wood mushrooms includes rusty brown spore print, small brown hygrophanous cap, wood growth, and a ring or ring zone that may disappear.

records.poisoning_effects: False morels should be treated as poisonous. Some Gyromitra contain gyromitrin, which can convert to monomethylhydrazine; poisoning has caused severe illness and deaths.
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat in this guide. Included only as a morel look-alike caution record.
records.field_identification: Compare every morel-like mushroom by cutting it lengthwise. True morels are hollow inside and have pitted caps; false morels may be wrinkled, ridged, wavy, brain-like, free-hanging at the cap, cottony, chambered, or solid rather than cleanly hollow.
records.overview: Caution stub for false morel groups, especially Gyromitra and Verpa species, because they can appear at similar times and places as true morels in Michigan.
records.look_alike_notes: Do not rely on color alone. Check the cap attachment and interior structure. Free-hanging caps, solid or chambered stems, and brain-like or wrinkled caps are warning signs.

mushroom_profile.season_note: April through June, overlapping true morel season; Michigan DNR notes false morels occur at the same times and places as true morels.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Gyromitra / false morels
use_links.notes: False morel group traits and hollow-vs-not-hollow comparison.

records.mushroom_card_note: Wrinkled/lobed false morel caution record; do not treat as a true morel.
records.edibility_detail: Poisonous / avoid in this guide. False morels are not one safe species: several Gyromitra, Verpa, Discina, and Helvella-like morel look-alikes can cause severe poisoning, and some contain gyromitrin/MMH-related toxins. This app treats all false-morel candidates as reject mushrooms unless an expert has identified the exact species and local legal/food-safety guidance supports use.
records.toxicity_notes: Morel-like mushroom with brainy, wavy, lobed, cottony, chambered, or not-cleanly-hollow interior should be rejected for this guide. Slice every morel-like mushroom lengthwise.

records.poisoning_effects: Contains amatoxins; poisoning symptoms can be delayed and can progress to liver and kidney failure, coma, and death. Cooking, freezing, boiling, or drying does not make it safe.
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat. Included only as a deadly Amanita caution record.
records.field_identification: Amanita with white free gills, white spore print, ring on the stalk, and sack-like volva/cup at the base. Cap color is variable and can be greenish, yellowish, brownish, tan, or rarely white, so color alone is not a safe ID feature.
records.overview: Deadly Amanita caution stub for records that mention death caps or deadly Amanitas as look-alikes.
records.look_alike_notes: The volva/cup at the base may be partly underground. Dig up the whole base for identification and do not rely on cap color or taste.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Amanita phalloides
use_links.notes: Death cap identifying traits including volva, ring, white gills, and white spore print.

use_links.label: Britannica: Death cap
use_links.notes: Toxins, delayed symptoms, and fatal poisoning risk.

records.mushroom_card_note: Deadly Amanita: cap, free gills, ring, and volva/cup at base.
records.edibility_detail: Deadly poisonous Amanita. Do not eat, taste, or mix with food mushrooms. Death cap contains amatoxins that can cause delayed gastrointestinal illness followed by liver and kidney failure; cooking, drying, or freezing must not be treated as making it safe.
records.toxicity_notes: Deadly amatoxin risk. Included as a high-severity caution record; Upper Michigan local occurrence was not verified in this re-audit, so the record should be treated as general Amanita safety education rather than a confirmed common U.P. species.

records.edibility_detail: Some Lepiota species are amatoxin-containing and potentially fatal; avoid small parasol/lepiotoid mushrooms unless identified by an expert.
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat. Included as a dangerous look-alike/safety group, not as a food species.
records.medicinal_uses: No reliable medicinal use is recorded for this app entry unless noted in Other uses or source links.
records.other_uses: No practical non-food use is recorded for this app entry yet.
records.overview: Safety-group entry for small lepiotoid mushrooms because some Lepiota species contain amatoxins and can cause fatal poisoning.
records.notes: Safety-group entry for small lepiotoid mushrooms because some Lepiota species contain amatoxins and can cause fatal poisoning.
records.general_notes: Safety-group entry for small lepiotoid mushrooms because some Lepiota species contain amatoxins and can cause fatal poisoning.
records.field_identification: Small to medium white or brown-scaled gilled mushrooms with a ring or ring zone; species-level ID often requires microscopy. Treat small Lepiota-like mushrooms as no-eat unless expert-confirmed.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Lepiotoid mushrooms
use_links.notes: Lepiotoid identification overview and macroscopic characters.

use_links.label: PMC: amatoxins in Lepiota
use_links.notes: Documents fatal-poisoning risk from amatoxin-containing Lepiota species.

records.poisoning_effects: Some Lepiota species are amatoxin-containing and potentially fatal; avoid small parasol/lepiotoid mushrooms unless identified by an expert.

records.edibility_detail: Deadly-risk small brown mushroom group associated with amatoxin-type poisoning concerns; not suitable for casual foraging.
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat. Treat small brown conical mushrooms as unsafe without expert identification.
records.medicinal_uses: No reliable medicinal use is recorded for this app entry unless noted in Other uses or source links.
records.other_uses: No practical non-food use is recorded for this app entry yet.
records.overview: Safety-group entry for small brown lawn/woodchip mushrooms because Pholiotina rugosa/Conocybe filaris-type mushrooms are deadly-risk look-alikes.
records.notes: Safety-group entry for small brown lawn/woodchip mushrooms because Pholiotina rugosa/Conocybe filaris-type mushrooms are deadly-risk look-alikes.
records.general_notes: Safety-group entry for small brown lawn/woodchip mushrooms because Pholiotina rugosa/Conocybe filaris-type mushrooms are deadly-risk look-alikes.
records.field_identification: Tiny to small brown gilled mushrooms with conical to bell-shaped caps, fragile stems, rusty-brown gills/spores; ring may be present in Pholiotina rugosa. Confident ID often requires microscopy.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Pholiotina rugosa
use_links.notes: Notes terrestrial habitat, persistent pale ring, and need for microscopic features.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Conocybe and Pholiotina
use_links.notes: Conocybe overview: small conical caps, fragile stems, rusty brown mature gills/spore print.

records.poisoning_effects: Deadly-risk small brown mushroom group associated with amatoxin-type poisoning concerns; not suitable for casual foraging.

records.edibility_detail: Poisonous; causes significant gastrointestinal illness and is too risky as a casual edible-parasol look-alike.
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat. Common cause of severe gastrointestinal poisoning; included as a caution record.
records.medicinal_uses: No reliable medicinal use is recorded for this app entry unless noted in Other uses or source links.
records.other_uses: No practical non-food use is recorded for this app entry yet.
records.overview: Important poisonous lawn mushroom and parasol look-alike. The greenish spore print is the key field clue when mature specimens are available.
records.notes: Important poisonous lawn mushroom and parasol look-alike. The greenish spore print is the key field clue when mature specimens are available.
records.general_notes: Important poisonous lawn mushroom and parasol look-alike. The greenish spore print is the key field clue when mature specimens are available.
records.field_identification: Large white parasol-type mushroom in lawns or grassy places, often in rings; mature gills and spore print become greenish. Young white-gilled specimens are not safe to separate casually from edible parasols.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Chlorophyllum molybdites
use_links.notes: Greenish spore print, greenish old gills, lawn/fairy-ring habitat.

use_links.label: Missouri Dept. of Conservation: Green-spored Lepiota
use_links.notes: Large white lawn mushroom with greenish gills/spores and ring.

records.poisoning_effects: Poisonous; causes significant gastrointestinal illness and is too risky as a casual edible-parasol look-alike.

records.edibility_detail: Poisonous-risk group; muscarine poisoning is associated with Inocybe and Clitocybe-type mushrooms.
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat. Included as a caution group for muscarine-type poisoning risk.
records.medicinal_uses: No reliable medicinal use is recorded for this app entry unless noted in Other uses or source links.
records.other_uses: No practical non-food use is recorded for this app entry yet.
records.overview: Safety-group entry for muscarine-risk gilled mushrooms that can look plain and harmless but are not beginner edibles.
records.notes: Safety-group entry for muscarine-risk gilled mushrooms that can look plain and harmless but are not beginner edibles.
records.general_notes: Safety-group entry for muscarine-risk gilled mushrooms that can look plain and harmless but are not beginner edibles.
records.field_identification: Small to medium brown, white, or pale gilled mushrooms; Inocybe often has silky/fibrous caps and brown spores, while Clitocybe-type mushrooms often have pale spores and attached to decurrent gills. Avoid this entire risk group as food.

mushroom_profile.spore_print: Brown, white, cream, or pinkish depending on genus

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Inocybe
use_links.notes: Inocybe overview: brown spore prints, conical/silky/scaly caps, cryptic species.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Clitocyboid mushrooms
use_links.notes: Clitocyboid overview: gills attached/decurrent, pale to pinkish spore prints.

use_links.label: MykoWeb: muscarine poisoning group
use_links.notes: Notes muscarine poisoning primarily in Clitocybe and Inocybe.

records.poisoning_effects: Poisonous-risk group; muscarine poisoning is associated with Inocybe and Clitocybe-type mushrooms.
